---
title: "Sphere Grids"
---

Sphere grids organize finite circular rows into latitude-like and longitude-like coordinates. The finite grid gives the reader a way to talk about poles, equators, rows, columns, and antipodal pairing without claiming the full continuous sphere has been formalized.

Use this page as a vocabulary bridge from S1 loops to S2 grid structure. The grid language is useful only when the finite model and proof status stay visible.

::: {.warning-box}
This page does not claim continuous sphere geometry beyond the finite models represented by theorem ids.
:::

## What To Notice

- Latitude-like rows organize circular address layers.
- Longitude-like addresses reuse S1 circular indexing.
- Antipodal and pole/equator claims need explicit theorem status.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

Why is a finite sphere grid useful for learning, even though it is not the same as a continuous sphere?

:::

## Source Trail

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="S2-T0003"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="S2-0002"></div>

<p data-paper-ref="papers/S2/PAPER_S2_02_SPHERE_GRIDS_LATITUDE_COILS.md">Paper source: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/S2/PAPER_S2_02_SPHERE_GRIDS_LATITUDE_COILS.md">S2 Sphere Grids And Latitude Coils</a></p>

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
